questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Timeline view in Notion?

options.text: To display database entries in alphabetical order
options.explanation: Timeline views organize items chronologically, not alphabetically.

options.text: To show projects or items along a chronological axis
options.explanation: Correct – Timeline views are designed to display projects chronologically.

options.text: To create a visual mind‑map of related concepts
options.explanation: Mind‑maps are a different type of view; timelines focus on dates.

options.text: To list items in a simple bullet list
options.explanation: Bullet lists are a basic text format, not a timeline representation.

questions.question: Before you can use a Timeline view, what must be present in the database?

options.text: At least one text property
options.explanation: Text properties are optional; a date property is required for timelines.

options.text: A checkbox property for task completion
options.explanation: Checkboxes are useful but not required for a timeline.

options.text: At least one date property
options.explanation: Timelines need dates to position items along the time axis.

options.text: A relation property linking to another database
options.explanation: Relations can enhance timelines but are not a prerequisite.

questions.question: If you want to quickly set up a Timeline view, which of the following is the most efficient approach?

options.text: Manually add a new view and configure every property from scratch
options.explanation: While possible, this takes more time than using a ready-made template.

options.text: Start with a template from the Notion Template Gallery or the template picker
options.explanation: Templates provide a pre‑configured timeline, saving setup time.

options.text: Copy a board view and rename it to ‘Timeline’
options.explanation: A board view does not automatically become a timeline; the view type must be changed.

options.text: Import a CSV file containing dates and let Notion auto‑detect the timeline
options.explanation: CSV import creates a table, but you still need to switch to a timeline view manually.

questions.question: How can you visually organize timeline entries by a shared characteristic, such as project phase or team?

options.text: By adding a filter that hides all other entries
options.explanation: Filters hide items; they don’t group them visually.

options.text: By using the “Group by” option in the timeline’s menu
options.explanation: Grouping lets you cluster entries based on a selected property.

options.text: By changing the database’s default view to a table
options.explanation: Switching to a table removes the timeline visualization.

options.text: By renaming each entry to include the characteristic in its title
options.explanation: Renaming doesn’t provide visual grouping; it only changes text.

questions.question: What advantage do sub‑items provide when added to a timeline entry?

options.text: They allow you to assign multiple dates to the same entry
options.explanation: A single entry still has one date property; sub‑items are separate rows.

options.text: They let you break a larger project into more detailed tasks within the same timeline bar
options.explanation: Sub‑items create a hierarchical view, giving finer granularity inside a project.

options.text: They automatically generate Gantt‑style dependencies
options.explanation: Dependencies must be added explicitly; sub‑items alone don’t create them.

options.text: They change the timeline view into a Kanban board
options.explanation: Sub‑items affect hierarchy, not the view type.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the difference between Notion’s Timeline view and a traditional Gantt chart?

options.text: A timeline in Notion cannot show dependencies, whereas Gantt charts always do
options.explanation: Notion timelines can display dependencies when you add them.

options.text: Notion timelines are limited to a single project, while Gantt charts can handle multiple projects
options.explanation: A Notion timeline can include any number of entries, even across projects.

options.text: Notion timelines combine Gantt‑style scheduling with the ability to edit content directly on the page, offering broader workflow flexibility
options.explanation: Correct – Notion timelines go beyond static Gantt charts by allowing in‑page editing, grouping, sub‑items, and more.

options.text: Gantt charts are interactive, whereas Notion timelines are static images
options.explanation: Both are interactive; Notion timelines are live database views, not static images.

questions.question: When adding dependencies between timeline entries, what visual effect does Notion provide?

options.text: It draws arrows connecting the related bars on the timeline
options.explanation: Dependencies appear as arrows, showing how one entry relies on another.

options.text: It changes the color of dependent entries to red
options.explanation: Color changes are not the default visual cue for dependencies.

options.text: It adds a numeric label indicating the order of tasks
options.explanation: Numeric ordering isn’t automatically added by dependencies.

options.text: It collapses dependent entries into a single bar
options.explanation: Dependencies keep entries separate; they are linked, not merged.

questions.question: Which of the following actions would NOT be possible directly from a timeline’s menu?

options.text: Changing the timeline’s time scale (e.g., week to month)
options.explanation: Adjusting the time scale is a standard option in the timeline menu.

options.text: Grouping entries by a selected property
options.explanation: Grouping is available via the timeline’s menu.

options.text: Adding a new database property that isn’t a date
options.explanation: Creating new properties is done in the database settings, not the timeline view menu.

options.text: Switching the view type from Timeline to Table
options.explanation: You can change the view type from the view switcher, which is part of the view menu.
